skill.name: Startup Financial Modeling
skill.description: Build comprehensive 3-5 year financial models with revenue projections, cost structures, cash flow analysis, and scenario planning for early-stage startups.
skill.summary: Guidance for creating startup financial models with cohort-based revenue projections, cost structures, and cash flow analysis.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Prompt-only skill with no executable code. No security risks detected. The skill provides educational content and guidance for startup financial modeling including revenue projections, cost structures, cash flow analysis, and scenario planning.

content.value_statement: Create comprehensive 3-5 year financial projections for early-stage startups. This skill provides structured guidance for revenue modeling, cost structures, cash flow analysis, and investor-ready fundraising scenarios.
actual_capabilities: Create cohort-based revenue projections using customer acquisition and retention curves
Build detailed cost structures with COGS, S&M, R&D, and G&A breakdowns
Calculate cash flow metrics including burn rate, runway, and funding requirements
Generate three-scenario financial models (conservative, base, optimistic)
Model unit economics including CAC, LTV, and CAC payback period
Create fundraising scenarios with dilution and milestone planning
limitations: Cannot execute financial calculations or generate spreadsheets directly
Does not access real-time market data or company financial information
Assumptions and projections require validation with actual business data
Cannot replace professional financial advice or audit services

use_cases.title: Pre-Seed Fundraising Preparation
use_cases.description: Founders preparing for their first institutional round can create realistic financial projections to demonstrate market understanding and growth potential to investors.

use_cases.title: Board Reporting and Planning
use_cases.description: Startup CFOs and finance leads can generate monthly or quarterly financial reports comparing actual performance against projections with variance analysis.

use_cases.title: Business Model Validation
use_cases.description: Entrepreneurs exploring new business models can model different revenue streams and cost structures to assess unit economics and scalability before launching.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Revenue Projection
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 3-year financial model for a SaaS startup starting with $0 revenue. We plan to acquire 50 new customers per month, charge $100 per month, and expect 80% annual retention. Include revenue projections and key metrics.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick revenue forecast for early planning

prompt_templates.title: Complete Financial Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a comprehensive financial model for my [business type]. We have [current metrics including customers, revenue, team size]. Project 3 years with monthly detail for year 1. Include revenue, costs by category (COGS, S&M, R&D, G&A), headcount plan, cash flow, and key metrics like CAC, LTV, and burn rate.
prompt_templates.scenario: Full financial model for fundraising or board meetings

prompt_templates.title: Scenario Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Create three scenarios (conservative, base, optimistic) for our financial projections. Base case: [your assumptions]. Vary customer acquisition by ±30%, churn by ±20%, and pricing by ±15% across scenarios. Show how runway and funding needs change.

prompt_templates.title: Unit Economics Deep Dive
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze our unit economics. Current metrics: CAC is $[amount], customers pay $[pricing] monthly, average customer lasts [months]. Calculate LTV, LTV/CAC ratio, and CAC payback period. Compare to industry benchmarks and suggest improvements if payback exceeds 18 months.
prompt_templates.scenario: Optimizing customer acquisition and retention strategy

output_examples.input: Create a SaaS financial model for year 1 with 100 new customers per month, $200 ARPU, and 75% annual retention.
output_examples.output: The model shows December MRR of $180,000 (900 paying customers × $200). Annual churn reduces cohorts to 75% retained by year-end. With estimated CAC of $4,000 and LTV of $6,000, the LTV/CAC ratio is 1.5x, indicating room for improvement in retention or pricing to reach the recommended 3x ratio.

output_examples.input: Calculate runway if we have $2M in cash and monthly burn of $150K.
output_examples.output: With $2M cash and $150K monthly burn, your runway is approximately 13 months. This assumes burn rate remains constant. If you plan to hire or increase marketing spend, factor in the higher burn rate which would reduce runway to approximately 10-11 months.

output_examples.input: Build a fundraising scenario for raising $3M at $15M pre-money valuation.
output_examples.output: Raising $3M at $15M pre-money results in $18M post-money valuation with 16.7% dilution. Recommended use of funds: Product Development $1.2M (40%), Sales & Marketing $1.2M (40%), G&A and Operations $300K (10%), Working Capital Buffer $300K (10%). This provides approximately 18-24 months of runway to reach key milestones.

best_practices: Always create three scenarios (conservative, base, optimistic) rather than relying on single-point projections
Use cohort-based revenue modeling rather than simple growth percentages for more accurate projections
Include fully-loaded compensation costs (salary × 1.3 to 1.4) when modeling headcount expenses
Validate assumptions against industry benchmarks and peer companies at similar stages
anti_patterns: Avoid aggressive hockey-stick projections without historical data or market validation to support them
Do not confuse revenue recognition with cash flow—model payment terms and collection timing separately
Never present a single scenario to investors without preparing conservative downside cases
Avoid static headcount assumptions that ignore hiring timelines, ramp periods, and attrition

faq.question: What is the difference between MRR and ARR?
faq.answer: MRR (Monthly Recurring Revenue) is the normalized revenue from subscriptions in a single month. ARR (Annual Recurring Revenue) is MRR multiplied by 12, representing the annualized value of your recurring revenue. Investors typically focus on ARR for SaaS company valuation.

faq.question: How long should my financial projections be?
faq.answer: Standard practice is 3-5 years. Years 1-2 should have monthly detail, Year 3 quarterly detail, and Years 4-5 annual high-level projections. This balances operational precision for near-term planning with strategic visibility for long-term growth.

faq.question: What is a good CAC payback period?
faq.answer: Best-in-class SaaS companies achieve CAC payback in under 12 months. The industry benchmark is under 18 months. If payback exceeds 18-24 months, you may need to improve retention, increase pricing, or reduce acquisition costs to maintain healthy unit economics.

faq.question: Should I include fundraising in my financial model?
faq.answer: Yes, if you are actively raising or planning to raise. Model the funding round as a cash inflow and show how it extends runway to reach key milestones. Include dilution calculations and use of funds breakdown for investor presentations.

faq.question: How do I calculate runway?
faq.answer: Runway equals your current cash balance divided by your average monthly burn rate. For example, $1.5M in cash with $120K monthly burn provides approximately 12.5 months of runway. Always add a buffer for unexpected expenses or slower revenue growth.

faq.question: What is the Rule of 40?
faq.answer: The Rule of 40 is a SaaS benchmark where your revenue growth rate plus profit margin should equal at least 40. For example, a company growing 60% annually with -20% margins scores 40 (60 + (-20) = 40). This metric balances growth and efficiency for healthy SaaS businesses.
